summary:
This file describes how the “diffdoc” product is packaged, run, and distributed.

What it is
- The project is called “diffdoc.”
- Its purpose is to turn code changes in a repository into plain-English business explanations.
- It is licensed for open use under MIT.

How people use it
- It is meant to be run from the command line.
- It provides two runnable tools:
  - `diffdoc`
  - `diffdoc-mcp`
- These are the main entry points users or other systems would launch.

What gets shipped
- Only the built output and a few support files are included in the published package:
  - compiled code in `dist`
  - the README
  - the license
  - an example configuration file
- This means users get the finished version, not the source code.

System requirements
- It requires a recent version of Node.js, version 22 or newer.
- If someone tries to run it on an older environment, it may not work.

How it is built and started
- `build` compiles the TypeScript source into runnable JavaScript.
- `clean` removes the generated output folder.
- `start` builds the project and then runs it.
- `prepare` automatically builds the project before publishing or installing.

What it depends on
- It uses tools for:
  - command-line handling
  - talking to OpenAI
  - reading git history and repository changes
  - storing and searching information
  - validating data
  - supporting a model-context interface
- In business terms, this means the product is designed to inspect code changes, process them intelligently, and present results in a structured way.

Visible outcome for users
- A user can install and run the tool from the command line.
- The tool will analyze repository changes and translate them into business-friendly language.
- The package is set up so the published version is ready to use without exposing the source files.
